question: What does Dependency Injection solve, and how do you choose an injection style in Spring?
shortAnswer: Dependency Injection means a class receives collaborators instead of locating or constructing them. Spring's container resolves the object graph and manages bean lifecycle, which makes dependencies explicit and implementations replaceable. I prefer constructor injection for required collaborators because it supports immutable fields and plain unit tests; setters can represent genuinely optional reconfiguration. Field injection works, but hides requirements and couples tests to reflection or a container.
detailedAnswer:
Dependency Injection is one form of Inversion of Control. A service declares what it needs and the composition root/container decides which objects satisfy those needs. The benefit is not the `@Autowired` annotation itself; it is separation between business behavior and object construction.

In Spring:
- A component with one constructor can receive beans without annotating that constructor.
- Constructor injection exposes required dependencies, permits `final` fields and fails early when the graph cannot be built.
- Setter injection can model a dependency that is truly optional or replaceable, though optional dependencies should be used deliberately.
- Field injection is concise but conceals the construction contract and makes non-container tests less direct.
- `@Qualifier`, `@Primary` or an explicit `@Bean` method resolves intentional ambiguity between multiple candidates.

Depending on an interface is useful when there are meaningful alternatives or a boundary to protect, but creating an interface for every class is not required for DI. Scope and lifecycle matter too: injecting a request-scoped or prototype dependency into a singleton may require a proxy/provider rather than assuming a fresh object appears on every use.
keyPoints: DI separates object construction from business behavior
Constructor injection makes required dependencies explicit
Interfaces are useful at real boundaries, not mandatory for every bean
Qualifiers and explicit configuration resolve multiple candidates
Bean scope is part of the dependency contract
redFlags: Defining DI only as using `@Autowired`
Constructing infrastructure collaborators inside business methods
Using field injection while claiming the class is easy to instantiate in isolation
Ignoring scope when injecting shorter-lived beans into singletons

followUps.question: What happens when two beans implement the same interface?
followUps.hint: Make the selection explicit with a qualifier, primary bean, profile/condition or configuration method; ambiguity otherwise fails startup.

followUps.question: Would you create an interface only so Mockito can mock a dependency?
followUps.hint: Not necessarily. Define interfaces for architectural boundaries or alternatives; modern mocking can substitute classes, and a test concern alone should not dictate every production abstraction.

realExperience.title: Transfer from Jakarta CDI
realExperience.description: Production CDI experience covers container-managed object graphs, scopes, qualifiers and substitution. Spring's bean-resolution rules and constructor-injection conventions are the framework-specific layer.

question: How does Spring Boot auto-configuration decide what to create, and how do you debug an unexpected bean?
shortAnswer: `@SpringBootApplication` enables auto-configuration. Boot imports candidate auto-configuration classes and evaluates conditions based on the classpath, properties, application type and existing beans. Many defaults use `@ConditionalOnMissingBean`, so user configuration can make them back off, but that is not true of every bean. I inspect the condition-evaluation report and bean origin before excluding configuration or overriding it deliberately.
detailedAnswer:
Auto-configuration is conditional configuration, not runtime magic:

1. `@SpringBootApplication` includes `@EnableAutoConfiguration`.
2. Boot discovers auto-configuration candidates registered by libraries (modern Boot libraries use `META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports`).
3. Conditions such as `@ConditionalOnClass`, `@ConditionalOnProperty`, `@ConditionalOnWebApplication` and `@ConditionalOnMissingBean` decide whether a configuration or bean applies.
4. Configuration properties bind external values into typed objects.
5. User-defined beans make an auto-configuration back off only when its conditions say so; defining any bean does not globally override Boot.

For diagnosis, start the application with debug condition reporting or inspect the Actuator conditions endpoint in a secured environment. Check which condition matched, where the bean definition came from and whether configuration order/type information affected `@ConditionalOnMissingBean`. Prefer a targeted user bean or property first. Exclude an auto-configuration only when the whole feature is inappropriate, because exclusion also removes its other defaults.
keyPoints: Auto-configuration imports candidates and evaluates conditions
Classpath presence alone is only one possible condition
Back-off behavior depends on explicit missing-bean conditions
The condition report explains positive and negative matches
Targeted overrides are usually narrower than excluding an entire auto-configuration
redFlags: Calling auto-configuration magic without explaining conditions
Confusing component scanning with auto-configuration imports
Assuming every auto-configured bean backs off for any user bean
Excluding a configuration before inspecting why it matched

followUps.question: How can you disable one auto-configuration?
followUps.hint: Use the annotation exclusion or `spring.autoconfigure.exclude`, but first verify that a narrower property or bean override does not fit better.

followUps.question: Why should `@ConditionalOnMissingBean` be used carefully in custom auto-configuration?
followUps.hint: It sees bean definitions processed so far, so auto-configuration ordering and precise return types affect the result.

question: Where should a Spring transaction boundary be, and when is `REQUIRES_NEW` justified?
shortAnswer: I place the transaction around one database consistency boundary, usually a public service operation, and keep slow remote calls outside when possible. `REQUIRED` joins or creates the usual transaction. `REQUIRES_NEW` suspends it and starts an independent physical transaction, so I use it only when the inner result must commit independently and that partial outcome is acceptable. It needs another connection and can exhaust the pool under load.
detailedAnswer:
Propagation describes how a method participates when a transaction may already exist:

- `REQUIRED` joins the current transaction or creates one. An inner scope that marks rollback-only can cause `UnexpectedRollbackException` when the outer caller tries to commit.
- `REQUIRES_NEW` suspends the outer transaction and opens an independent physical transaction. Its commit survives an outer rollback, which is useful only when the domain accepts that split—for example, recording an attempt independently of a failed business operation. It does not guarantee that an audit survives process/database failure.
- `NESTED` typically uses JDBC savepoints inside one physical transaction and is not equivalent to `REQUIRES_NEW`; support depends on the transaction manager/resource.
- `SUPPORTS`, `MANDATORY`, `NOT_SUPPORTED` and `NEVER` express other participation constraints and should be used for a clear boundary, not memorized decoration.

In default proxy mode, only calls that pass through the proxy are advised; self-invocation does not start a different propagation. Imperative transactions are thread-bound and do not automatically flow to a newly created thread. By default, runtime exceptions and errors roll back, while checked exceptions require an explicit rule unless global defaults have been changed.

Keep the transaction short. A remote API call inside it holds locks/connections and cannot be made atomic merely by the annotation; outbox/saga/idempotency patterns may be more appropriate.
keyPoints: Transaction boundaries follow a business consistency unit
REQUIRES_NEW commits independently and changes failure semantics
REQUIRES_NEW needs another connection while the outer one remains bound
Default proxy mode does not advise self-invocation
Remote calls are not made transactional by `@Transactional`
redFlags: Using REQUIRES_NEW as a generic fix for rollback problems
Ignoring independent-commit inconsistency and pool sizing
Expecting self-invocation to apply a new propagation mode
Holding a database transaction open across unbounded network work

followUps.question: Why can REQUIRES_NEW exhaust a connection pool?
followUps.hint: The outer transaction keeps its connection while each thread requests another for the independent inner transaction.

followUps.question: What rolls back by default when a Spring transactional method throws a checked exception?
followUps.hint: The traditional default does not roll back for checked exceptions; configure a rollback rule or a global all-exceptions policy when that is the intended contract.

followUps.question: How would you publish an event only after a database change commits?
followUps.hint: A transactional outbox is robust across crashes; an after-commit callback alone still has a publish failure window.

realExperience.title: Transfer from Jakarta EE container-managed transactions
realExperience.description: Production CMT experience includes REQUIRED/REQUIRES_NEW-style propagation and using NOT_SUPPORTED around orchestration with remote systems. Spring proxy behavior and rollback configuration are framework-specific differences.

question: How do you design consistent and safe error handling for a Spring Boot HTTP API?
shortAnswer: I centralize transport mapping with `@RestControllerAdvice` or `ResponseEntityExceptionHandler`, while domain code throws domain-level failures rather than HTTP exceptions. Each failure maps to a status based on API semantics and a stable machine-readable problem type/code. Spring's `ProblemDetail` supports RFC 9457. I expose actionable client detail, keep stack traces and internals in secured logs, and attach a correlation identifier.
keyPoints: Keep domain failures independent from HTTP transport types
Centralize mappings and use stable machine-readable problem identifiers
Choose status codes from the specific HTTP semantics
Do not expose stack traces or sensitive diagnostic data
Test status, body contract and content type for error paths
redFlags: Returning 500 for every failure
Returning an exception message or stack trace directly
Forcing clients to parse localized human text
Catching the same exception independently in every controller

followUps.question: What should a validation-error response contain?
followUps.hint: A stable problem type plus structured violations such as field/path and code; avoid exposing rejected secrets or relying only on prose.

followUps.question: When is 409 Conflict more appropriate than 400?
followUps.hint: When the representation is understood but cannot be applied because it conflicts with the current state, such as a stale version or invalid state transition.

realExperience.title: Transfer from production HTTP error contracts
realExperience.description: Production JAX-RS error mapping and domain-exception experience transfers to status selection, safe payloads and centralized handling. Spring ProblemDetail and controller-advice behavior are the framework-specific layer.

question: How do you diagnose an N+1 problem in Spring Data JPA and choose a fix that still paginates correctly?
clarifyingQuestions: Which repository call and relationship access generate the repeated queries?
Is the endpoint returning entities, projections or a paginated DTO?
How many SQL statements and result rows does one request produce?
shortAnswer: I confirm N+1 from one request's SQL count rather than changing mappings blindly. For a bounded to-one graph I may use an entity graph or fetch join; for paginated to-many data I often page parent IDs first and fetch children separately, use batch fetching, or return a projection. I compare query count and row multiplication because one giant fetch join can be slower or make pagination incorrect.
detailedAnswer:
Spring Data offers several fetch-plan tools, each with limits:

- `@EntityGraph` or JPQL `join fetch` can load required associations for a particular repository method.
- Batch fetching reduces repeated lazy loads without joining every child into one result.
- Interface/class DTO projections select the fields needed by a read endpoint and avoid exposing managed entities.
- A two-step query can fetch a page of parent IDs, then load needed relationships for those IDs.

A collection fetch join with `Pageable` is risky because SQL rows represent parent-child pairs, not distinct parents. Depending on query/provider, pagination can be performed in memory or produce an unexpected page. Fetching multiple to-many collections can multiply rows and may hit provider-specific multiple-bag limits.

Detection should use SQL/query counts in a test or APM trace. Add an integration regression test that executes the access pattern and, where practical, asserts a query-count budget or at least verifies the intended fetch plan. Avoid globally setting relationships to EAGER; it applies to unrelated use cases and can create a different performance problem.
keyPoints: Confirm the repeated access pattern before changing mappings
Entity graphs and fetch joins are per-use-case tools
To-many fetch joins need special care with Pageable
Batching, projections and two-step loading preserve bounded result sets
Measure result rows and memory as well as query count
redFlags: Changing every relationship to EAGER
Fetch-joining multiple collections without measuring row growth
Combining a to-many fetch join with pagination without verifying generated SQL
Claiming N+1 is fixed solely because one trace got faster

followUps.question: Why can `join fetch` plus `Pageable` be incorrect or inefficient?
followUps.hint: The SQL limit applies to joined rows rather than distinct parent entities, so providers may warn, paginate in memory or produce surprising pages.

followUps.question: How would you keep this performance bug from returning?
followUps.hint: Add an integration test for the real access path, observe query count/shape, and keep APM alerts or dashboards for endpoint latency and database calls.

realExperience.title: Transfer from Hibernate/JPA performance work
realExperience.description: For about 26,705 enrollment records in a period, a listing generated roughly 48,000 relationship queries. Projections, pagination and query restructuring reduced it to about 10 queries and improved latency from about 16 minutes to about 2 seconds. The diagnosis transfers directly; Spring Data annotations are the framework-specific interface.

question: When do you use a plain unit test, a Spring test slice and `@SpringBootTest`?
shortAnswer: I use plain JUnit/Mockito for business logic that does not need container behavior. A slice such as `@WebMvcTest` or `@DataJpaTest` loads a focused part of Spring to verify MVC/security or persistence wiring. `@SpringBootTest` is for a smaller number of flows that need the full application context. I choose the narrowest scope that can reveal the failure and use the production database engine when database behavior matters.
detailedAnswer:
The annotations answer different questions:

- **Plain unit test**: instantiate the class and supply fakes/mocks. Best for fast policy and transformation tests; loading Spring here adds time without confidence.
- **`@WebMvcTest`**: focused MVC infrastructure such as controllers, serialization, validation and configured web security. Collaborating services are typically supplied as test doubles. It does not prove repository/database behavior.
- **`@DataJpaTest`**: focused repositories, mappings and persistence behavior. Configure a real PostgreSQL container when dialect, native SQL or migrations matter; an embedded database is not inherently wrong for database-agnostic tests, but it cannot approve PostgreSQL-specific behavior.
- **`@SpringBootTest`**: full application configuration, useful for selected cross-layer integration or startup checks. It is slower and a passing context-load test alone proves little.

Testcontainers can provide controlled infrastructure, while Spring Boot service connections can simplify dynamic connection properties. State isolation still needs design. Transactional test rollback may not cover work performed in another thread or through a real server request using another transaction.

Avoid fixed test-percentage rules. Let risk determine scope, and keep failures diagnosable by not using the full context for every case.
keyPoints: Plain unit tests do not need a Spring context
Slices verify a focused framework boundary
SpringBootTest verifies selected full-context interactions
Use the real database engine for engine-specific claims
Transactional test annotations do not roll back every cross-thread/server effect
redFlags: Using SpringBootTest for every unit-level rule
Assuming WebMvcTest proves service or persistence behavior
Assuming an embedded database is identical to PostgreSQL
Relying on rollback without understanding transaction/thread boundaries

followUps.question: What does a WebMvcTest give you that a direct controller unit test does not?
followUps.hint: Real MVC request mapping, argument conversion, validation, serialization and configured filter/security behavior within the slice.

followUps.question: When can an integration test leave data behind despite `@Transactional`?
followUps.hint: When application work runs in another transaction or thread, such as a real HTTP server request or asynchronous processing.

realExperience.title: Transfer from JUnit, Mockito and CDI integration testing
realExperience.description: Production tests separate plain business-unit tests, CDI wiring tests and PostgreSQL-backed query tests. Spring slices and full-context annotations apply the same scope decision through Spring-specific tooling.

question: How do you make Spring Boot configuration safe and predictable across environments?
shortAnswer: I keep non-secret defaults with the application, bind related settings into validated `@ConfigurationProperties`, and supply environment-specific values through the deployment platform. I do not summarize Spring's precedence as a four-item rule: it has a documented property-source order, and command-line, system, environment and config-data sources can override one another. I test the effective configuration and manage secrets with a dedicated secret mechanism and rotation plan.
detailedAnswer:
A robust approach separates ownership and validates the final result:

- Keep safe, portable defaults in packaged configuration. Use profile-specific documents only for coherent groups of settings, not as a secret store or a copy of every environment.
- Bind structured settings with `@ConfigurationProperties` and Bean Validation so a missing URL, invalid duration or negative pool size fails startup with a useful message.
- Follow Spring Boot's documented property-source precedence. Command-line arguments have high precedence over config data, while system properties and environment variables can override packaged files; tests and developer tools add other sources. Diagnose the effective origin rather than relying on a simplified remembered list.
- Use Kubernetes ConfigMaps or a config service for ordinary configuration and a secret manager/platform Secret with least privilege for credentials. A Kubernetes Secret is not encrypted merely because it is base64 encoded.
- Decide whether changes require restart, context refresh or dynamic lookup. Environment variables do not update inside a running process; mounted files may update but the application must reload them safely.
- Keep immutable environment differences outside the artifact and promote the same artifact digest between stages.

Profiles are not a substitute for testing combinations. Add startup/contract tests for required production configuration and expose only safe diagnostic metadata.
keyPoints: Use typed, validated configuration properties
Rely on documented property-source precedence, not an oversimplified list
Separate ordinary configuration from secret management
Define restart or reload behavior for rotated values
Promote the same artifact with externalized environment settings
redFlags: Committing credentials in profile files
Using unvalidated string lookups throughout business code
Assuming changed environment variables update a running JVM
Using profiles to hide untested combinations and precedence conflicts

followUps.question: How do you find which property source supplied an unexpected value?
followUps.hint: Use safe configuration diagnostics/Actuator origin information in a secured environment and inspect deployment inputs; do not expose secret values.

followUps.question: Can a Kubernetes Secret rotate without restarting the application?
followUps.hint: It depends on delivery: environment variables require restart, mounted content may update, and the application/client must support safe reload or lease renewal.

realExperience.title: Transfer from MicroProfile Config and OKD
realExperience.description: Production deployments combine packaged safe defaults with OKD-provided environment configuration. Spring Boot adds its own binding and precedence model, which must be learned and tested explicitly.

question: How would you expose Spring Boot Actuator safely and use it for Kubernetes health and observability?
shortAnswer: I expose only the endpoints operations actually needs, secure them by network and authorization policy, and avoid publishing sensitive environment or heap data. Actuator provides health groups for liveness and readiness and Micrometer-backed metrics. Liveness should reflect internal recoverability, not a shared dependency. Readiness dependency checks need a system-level decision because making every replica unready can remove all Service endpoints.
detailedAnswer:
Actuator endpoints are operational APIs, not automatically public dashboards. By default, Spring Boot exposes only health over HTTP/JMX; additional endpoints must be enabled/exposed deliberately and secured. Defining a custom `SecurityFilterChain` also means the application owns the Actuator access rules. Endpoints such as `env`, `configprops`, loggers and heap dump can reveal or alter sensitive state even when some values are sanitized.

For Kubernetes, Boot exposes health groups such as `/actuator/health/liveness` and `/actuator/health/readiness` from application availability state. Liveness should not depend on an external database: restart cannot repair it and simultaneous restarts amplify failure. Boot does not add external health indicators to readiness by default. Add a dependency only after deciding that an instance with that dependency unavailable should receive no traffic; a shared failure can otherwise make every endpoint unready.

If management runs on a separate port/context, its probe can succeed while the main web connector is broken. Exposing additional probe paths on the main server port can test the same infrastructure.

Metrics use Micrometer and can be exported to a monitoring system, where request latency distributions, errors, JVM/GC, pools and business signals need useful tags without unbounded cardinality. Health indicates current serving state; metrics and alerts explain trends and degradation. Do not include secrets, user IDs or per-request values as metric tags.
keyPoints: Expose and authorize only required Actuator endpoints
Liveness represents internal recoverability, not shared dependency health
Readiness dependency checks can remove every replica from traffic
Probe the main serving path when a separate management port could hide failure
Control metric-tag cardinality and sensitive data
redFlags: Exposing all Actuator endpoints publicly
Adding a shared database to liveness
Assuming the management port proves the main HTTP port is healthy
Using user IDs, URLs with identifiers or request IDs as metric tags

followUps.question: Why does Spring Boot omit extra dependency indicators from readiness by default?
followUps.hint: A shared dependency failure could make every application instance unready and remove all routing targets; inclusion is a system-specific choice.

followUps.question: Which Actuator endpoints would you expose to the public internet?
followUps.hint: Usually none directly; even health detail should be minimized and access routed through the platform's internal probe/monitoring controls.

realExperience.title: Transfer from MicroProfile Health in OKD
realExperience.description: Production health endpoints distinguish application liveness from traffic readiness in OKD. Actuator health groups and Micrometer are the Spring-specific implementation to validate.
